# 🇱🇰 This Week in Sri Lanka

![LastUpdated](https://img.shields.io/badge/last_updated-2026--05--29_06:56:22-green)
[![RSS](https://img.shields.io/badge/RSS-Feed-orange)](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nuuuwan/lk_news_digest/refs/heads/main/rss.xml)
[![Broadsheet](https://img.shields.io/badge/Broadsheet-View-darkred)](https://nuuuwan.github.io/lk_news_digest)

*Generated by gpt-5 from 304 English News Articles published between 2026-05-22 and 2026-05-29.*

## 1. IMF Executive Board approved Sri Lanka’s fifth and sixth reviews under the Extended Fund Facility, clearing roughly $695 million.

The decision increased cumulative disbursements to <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">about $2.4 billion</span> under the four‑year arrangement for balance‑of‑payments support (a multiyear programme that releases tranches as reforms advance). The lender’s staff projected <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">growth of close to 3% in 2026</span>, weaker than earlier estimates, citing higher energy costs and softer tourism after the Middle East conflict.

Price pressures were expected to average near 5% this year, with official reserves envisaged to reach <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">around $8.6 billion</span> by end‑2026. The review flagged the need to keep revenue measures on track, advance <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">power sector restructuring</span>, and tighten public financial management, while noting that performance against quantitative targets at end‑2025 remained broadly on course.

## 2. Central Bank lifted the Overnight Policy Rate by 100 basis points to 8.75%.

The monetary authority cited wider risks to price stability and the external account after fuel‑driven cost increases and a weaker currency. Headline inflation rose to <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">5.4% in April</span> on the latest reading, reversing earlier disinflation, while private‑sector credit had begun to expand faster than expected.

Gross buffers stood near <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">$6.8 billion</span> at end‑April despite continued debt service. The new stance aims to cool demand and ease second‑round effects from energy tariff adjustments, with the corridor for the <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">Standing Deposit and Lending facilities</span> (the floor and ceiling around the policy rate) reset at 8.25% and 9.25%.

## 3. Attorney General told the Supreme Court that indictments would be filed within a month against the former police chief over the 2022 Galle Face attacks.

The timeline was placed on record during an appeal relating to an earlier order that had quashed a directive to list the official as a suspect. A <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">three‑judge bench</span> <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">fixed the matter for further hearing on 2 September</span>, following submissions by state counsel and the defence.

The case concerns violence at the main protest site during the ‘Aragalaya’ movement. An indictment (a <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">formal criminal charge</span> filed in a higher court) would render some pending proceedings unnecessary, senior defence counsel argued while reserving the right to respond once charges are served.

## 4. Cabinet approved the ‘Surakimu Lanka’ National Energy Security Mission to shift demand and cut import exposure.

The programme targets load management toward periods with stronger renewable output and promotes efficiency through behavioural change. Implementation will be coordinated across the <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">Presidential Secretariat</span>, the Energy Ministry, the <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">Sustainable Energy Authority</span>, and a dedicated Clean Sri Lanka unit.

Officials framed the plan as a response to hydro shortfalls, global price spikes, and supply disruptions linked to geopolitics. The framework blends public messaging, tariff‑design tools, and institutional coordination to curb peak demand and <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">reduce reliance on thermal generation</span>.

## 5. Election Commission said it can conduct Provincial Council polls once Parliament removes legal hurdles.

At a meeting of the <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">select committee on the electoral system</span>, officials from the justice arm outlined obstacles and potential remedies. The poll authority confirmed <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">operational readiness</span> under any framework enacted on the committee’s recommendation.

Lawmakers were told that pending clarifications include franchise provisions and constituency design. The chair indicated a <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">report would go to the full House</span> after consultations with legal advisors.

## 6. Police said efforts are under way to repatriate 89 fugitives listed on Interpol Red Notices.

A spokesperson reported that <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">eight were returned</span> from the United Arab Emirates on 27 May after an intelligence‑led operation, following 21 arrivals earlier in the month. Since 2024, <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">32 wanted persons</span> have been brought back and handed to specialist units.

A Red Notice (an <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">international request to locate</span> and provisionally detain for extradition) remains active for the remaining suspects linked to <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">organised crime and narcotics</span>.

## 7. State petroleum operator said fuel stocks are adequate through end‑July as price review looms.

Management confirmed arrivals of <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">two crude cargoes</span> on 28 and 31 May and said all shipments scheduled for the month had discharged. Officials added that a <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">pricing decision would be taken at month‑end</span> in light of international markets and exchange movements.

The company urged motorists to avoid panic buying and noted that allocation controls via the <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">digital quota remain in place</span>. A separate briefing indicated no change had been approved to tariffs at the time of the statement.

## 8. Supreme Court held that a lender may proceed directly against a guarantor where a waiver clause permits it.

Dismissing an appeal, the bench affirmed a commercial court ruling that <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">enforced a personal undertaking</span> after credit facilities were defaulted by the principal obligor. The judgment turned on an <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">express term allowing action</span> without first suing the borrower.

The court also rejected a defence that documents had been signed in blank, saying such a plea requires special proof, for example illiteracy or incapacity. The decision clarifies enforcement where guarantees contain ‘no‑sue‑first’ language (a <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">contractual waiver of the benefit of discussion</span>).

## 9. Twenty‑one alleged gang figures deported from Dubai arrived under escort and were handed to investigators.

Flights were <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">diverted to Mattala</span> due to weather, police said, identifying two high‑profile returnees earmarked for questioning by central units. Authorities said case files cover <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">serious offences</span>, including racketeering and drug trafficking.

Further screenings and charge decisions will follow after statements are recorded. Additional removals are being coordinated with foreign counterparts.

## 10. Police warned motorists about fake traffic‑fine messages mimicking the GovPay platform.

Officers urged the public not to click links in unsolicited texts and to pay only via <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">official gateways</span> after a paper ticket is issued. The scam involved a <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">bogus site designed to harvest card data</span> by posing as the state’s e‑payment portal for penalties.

GovPay (the Government’s online settlement system for fixed penalties) requires a <span style="background-color:#EBEBEB;border-radius:3px;padding:0 2px">valid reference generated by an officer</span>. The cyber unit said takedown requests had been filed and asked victims to lodge complaints promptly.
